item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Listen, strange men building houses in the middle of the road is no basis for a system of culture. Supreme societal cohesion derives from interpersonal communication, not some farcical mid-boulevard construction site.

That being said, I'm not sure I'm getting the connection between the note at the end and the rest, beyond the cities being extensive. That seems to (maybe?) be an actual civilization, with sailing capabilities and the ability to brainwash/coerce the sailors into joining them in going…somewhere?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As an Australian, can concur. No one is going to call anything but 000 first, and if a strange guy was building a house in the middle of the road, I think an Aussie would place a regular call to the local police station rather than the emergency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3800 was initially discovered on 2/3/11 following a 112 call from Useless Loop, Western Australia with reports of "strange men building houses in the middle of the road".

The emergency number in Australia is 000. Apparently 112 will work from mobile phones, but I don't know any Australian that would use 112.

item.title: Re: Author post.
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-3800, with Site-93's resident house cat ("Big Ounce") in the foreground"

I can see that, yeah.

item.title: Re: Author post.
item.content:encoded: Although if you take a picture of something else, say your cat, or your room, and the print is incidentally in the frame, you would own the copyright of that image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd say a combination of all of those. Do I need to focus on one aspect over the others?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I think this needs more than a few edits here and there - it needs to be rebuilt from the ground up.

If you're going for general unease, what should be causing the unease? The uncanny valley humanoids? The lack of explanation of why they do what they do? The fact that they could expand and cover the whole world?

If this is based on The Rabbits, is the unease you're looking for the unease of looking at a potential colonisation attempt?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
General unease, I guess. I suppose test logs would be a good way to exemplify what kinds of tasks the boys overcome, but after thinking about it I'm not sure if that would be a good fit in the article.

I'll make some edits later today, so let me know what you think the article needs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It depends on what your goal is. How do you want your audience to react to this article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would be the best fix? Test logs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with the above - this is a fairly enervating version of the "exponential growth" trope that is prevalent on the site in various forms. It also misses an opportunity to explore the most interesting part of the anomaly - these things are able to cooperate on highly complex tasks without any obvious form of communication. However the article merely glosses over that with the inaccurate statement that the instances "lack any sort of culture".

item.title: Re: Author post.
item.content:encoded: Correct.

item.title: Re: Author post.
item.content:encoded:
Gotcha.

Follow up question: I actually own a framed copy of the print. If I were to take a picture of that and use it in the article, would that also violate copyright since it's also technically a picture of the work in question?

item.title: Re: Author post.
item.content:encoded: Probably would be wise.

item.title: Re: Author post.
item.content:encoded:
Oh. Whoops.

Do I need to remove it?

item.title: Re: Author post.
item.content:encoded:
Usage is non-commercial and non-profit, as far as I can tell

If you mean by your usage here, no it is not. The site is CC-BY-SA, and the Fair Use paradigm only applies if you are using an image to refer expressly to that image or its associated work, such as in a wikipedia article or a review.

item.title: Re: Author post.
item.content:encoded:
Image source is from Shaun Tan's website. Usage is non-commercial and non-profit, as far as I can tell.

I'm not image enforcement or anything, but as far as I can tell The Rabbits is under traditional copyright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good to hear - I was having some doubts about the note. Though I'll admit that I've never actually played Catan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The note leaves me wondering. Though honestly, the sense I get from this is some kind of cosmic game of Settlers of Catan or something, with these little dudes the meeples or whatever they call things nowadays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty interesting. The use of footnotes is a little bit too much. +1, for everything else

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's funny but I guess it would be awesome if the civilization could advance, like "Brass Age", Iron Age" and on…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't doing it for me. After the base description, nothing noteworthy happens that drew me further into the narrative. The anomaly, as expected, grows and grows, but the narrative stops dead in its tracks as it does so. This uses the endnote trope in a typical manner that's been done to death.

item.title: Author post.
item.content:encoded:
"The rabbits came many grandparents ago.

At first, we didn't know what to think. They looked a bit like us. They weren't many of them. Some were friendly.

But our old people warned us:

Be careful. They won't understand the right ways. They only know their own country."

Tonight's homie roller awards go to MrWrong, Jacob Conwell, deku_link, and everyone in chat who helped me out.

Image source is from Shaun Tan's website. Usage is non-commercial and non-profit, as far as I can tell.

New image is from wikimedia commons by Rob Waddington. I think this one's OK?
